AIMS RIC course engine

Agentic Research Assistant for Examinable Thesis Drafting

This course is framed as if a graduate researcher is using an AI research assistant to discover literature, refine a topic, generate robust scientific prompts, produce examinable chapter drafts, and then test why detector-only AI judgments are fragile in quality assurance.

Tool arsenal used by the research assistant

Semantic ScholarScholarly graph and paper search OpenAlexOpen bibliographic index COREOpen-access research papers arXivPreprints and technical papers Turnitin AI WritingAI-writing indicator, not proof GPTZeroChecker-style risk signal ZeroGPTDetector-style classroom comparison Scribbr AI DetectorDetector limitations discussion
Part 1

Literature Discovery, Topic Refinement, and Scientific Chapter Drafting

The assistant cleans symbolic topic input such as “Green hydrogen + power-to-x + green financing + Rwanda” into coherent scholarly language, ranks recent and relevant papers, reads abstract and conclusion sections, generates robust prompts, and executes examinable chapter drafts with citations.

1. Research topic input and title refinement

How to formulate a scientific research title

A strong graduate-level research title is not a collection of keywords. It tells the examiner what method will be used, what system is being studied, where the study is located, and what scientific constraint or angle makes the work worth examining.

Title = [Method] + [System] + [Context] + [Constraint/Angle]

Scientific topic intelligence mode

Choose how the research assistant should reason about the topic. Grounded mode keeps the topic tightly aligned to selected papers and synthesis. Exploratory mode uses the same evidence base but proposes sharper, innovative research directions that remain plausible for a Master’s thesis in mathematical sciences.

Every generated topic must answer four examiner questions: what system is studied, how it is analysed, in what context, and under what constraint or scientific angle.
  • Method: the analytical approach, such as optimisation, stochastic modelling, machine learning, techno-economic modelling, Bayesian inference, dynamical systems, simulation, game theory, or mixed-methods modelling.
  • System: the object of study, such as green hydrogen systems, Power-to-X pathways, renewable energy infrastructure, disease dynamics, financial risk, or climate-energy systems.
  • Context: the setting, such as Rwanda, East Africa, AIMS Rwanda, emerging energy systems, rural health systems, or Sub-Saharan Africa.
  • Constraint/Angle: the scientific lens, such as policy constraints, green finance, technology readiness, uncertainty, feasibility, resilience, or implementation pathways.

Weak titles usually concatenate keywords. Strong titles show the research design. For example, instead of “Green Hydrogen Power-to-X Rwanda Financing”, use “A Techno-Economic and Policy-Constrained Model for Green Hydrogen and Power-to-X Deployment in Rwanda.”

Enter a rough topic. A scientific title-refinement agent removes symbols, connects concepts coherently, and generates SMART-aligned graduate-level thesis titles rather than copying the input verbatim.

2. Literature crawler and ranked paper discovery

Choose the live scholarly sources to crawl. Add API keys only where a source requires or benefits from one. The crawler then searches the selected sources, deduplicates papers, ranks them by topic relevance, recency, and citation signal, and displays full abstracts when returned by the source.

Live crawler configuration

Keys are stored only in this browser session/local storage for class demonstration. For production deployment, keep keys in Vercel environment variables and proxy calls through serverless API functions.

no key
Best default free index for broad scholarly discovery. Used for ranking, abstracts, years and citation signal.
optional key
Improves scholarly graph search. May rate-limit without a key.
no key
Useful for DOI metadata, recent publications and publisher records.
key needed
Open access aggregation. Enable only after adding a CORE key.
server proxy only
arXiv has no API key requirement, but browser CORS blocks direct fetches. It is held as a backend-ready source for the Vercel API route and disabled in HTML preview to prevent NetworkError during class.
manual validation
Hybrid mode: Google Scholar opens as a manual verification tab for the same cleaned query. This avoids fragile scraping/CAPTCHA failure while keeping Scholar in the real research workflow.
Crawler not tested yet.
Crawler limit is capped at 30 papers and display paginates 10 papers per page. The relevance gate uses live API language metadata first, then a fallback text check. It searches concept bundles and displays only on-topic English records.
Relevance intelligence layer is ready. It filters for English records, checks concept coverage, penalises weak matches, and recommends the strongest papers for synthesis.
No search yet.

3. Paper insight agent: abstract, method, results and conclusion summary

After papers are selected, the reading agent reads one paper at a time or all selected papers. Each insight is a 100–150 word scientific summary covering abstract scope, inferred method, result signal, conclusion status and thesis relevance. It never fabricates a conclusion where the source metadata does not provide one.

0 papers read
No papers read yet.

4. Scientific synthesis with in-text citations

The synthesis agent uses only papers sent to the synthesis basket. It writes a fast, citation-grounded mini-review that is suitable for later chapter drafting.

No synthesis yet. Send papers to synthesis or auto-select the top five ranked papers.

5. Great prompts for scientific research

Choose the thesis components to generate. The prompt agent writes graduate-level, SMART-aligned, evidence-based prompts that can be executed immediately using the selected papers and synthesis. Each component has its own word target; if a box is left blank, the default global target is used.

Thesis template engine

Student and thesis metadata

These fields populate the AIMS essay/thesis title page, declaration, footer metadata and export views.

Default mode keeps the current graduate thesis structure. Switch to AIMS to encode the AIMS chapter order, house-style constraints and scientific subheadings directly into generated prompts.
Introduction: context, motivation, research question, objectives, contributions, roadmapBackground and Problem Statement: literature review, definitions, notation, formal problem statementMethodology / Model: assumptions, model or method, equations, implementation/dataAnalysis and Results: results, discussion, limitationsConclusion and Future Work: conclusion, future workReferences and Appendices: verified references, code/proofs/data dictionaries
Active model: DeepSeek-R1 Research

Front matter source text

Declaration, acknowledgements and dedication are generated only from user-provided keywords/text. The LLM may refine style, but it must not invent personal details. Abstract remains evidence-driven and generated from the synthesis and selected papers.

Waiting for generated prompt...

6. Examinable chapter draft output

Execution produces a scientific, examinable draft for the selected thesis components. View the same artefact as Word/PDF-style prose or LaTeX source, then export it in the format needed for review.

No draft yet.
Part 2

Detection Risk Lab: Five-Step AI-Checker and Humanizer Simulation

This module is for QA officers and assessors. It does not teach misconduct. It safely demonstrates how detector signals can change after common surface transformations, why “AI probability” is not proof, and why source accountability, version history, research logs, and viva checks are stronger evidence.

Risk lab input

Use the chapter draft generated in Part 1, or paste a participant draft. Select the LLM label used for the run. Each button advances one Lego stage and prints the output text for that stage.

Auto-ingest rule: stages 1 and 3 use the full chapter generated in Part 1 unless the instructor deliberately pastes a different participant draft below.
1 Baseline LLM draft2 Detector scan3 Humanizer transform4 Detector after humanizer5 QA evidence judgment
Waiting for risk-lab run...

AI checker signals and what changed

These are classroom signals, not accusations. A responsible QA workflow combines detector output with citation verification, writing-process evidence, viva questions, version history, and course policy.

Run a risk stage to update the checker panel.
AIMS Research & Innovation — professional course simulator for AI fluency, research writing, and academic quality assurance.
Part 3

A Full Research Essay from a Single Notebook

This Lego block turns the chapter material from Part 2 into a reproducible mathematical-sciences research notebook. The notebook behaves like an agentic analyst/data-scientist: it plans the thesis, generates methodology equations, writes executable Python, runs synthetic or uploaded data, and assembles a traceable examinable essay draft.

1. Notebook configuration

Build from the current Part 2 chapters. Choose the most versatile math-aware LLM for methodology, modelling, formulas, sampling logic, derivations, code generation and interpretation.

Notebook plan will appear here.

2. Notebook emulator

Run the notebook cells one at a time. This models a graduate researcher producing a traceable research artefact rather than a one-click black box.

AIMS Research NotebookKernel idle · awaiting cell execution
[ ] Cell output
Run a cell to start.

3. Math-aware GenAI coding lab: synthetic or uploaded data

This is the analyst/data-scientist agent for a Masters in Mathematical Sciences thesis. It writes transparent Python, can use synthetic data when no dataset is available, or read an uploaded CSV-like file, then explains the quantitative evidence without fabricating findings.

Generated Python and analysis output will appear here.
Part 4

Full Thesis Builder: LaTeX ↔ Word/PDF View

Participants can toggle the thesis artefact between LaTeX source and a Word/PDF-style view. Export buttons download the current artefact as .tex, .doc-compatible HTML, or print-to-PDF.

1. Build thesis artefact from notebook + chapters

This section reuses Part 2 chapter drafts and Part 3 notebook synthesis. It does not replace Parts 1–2; it joins them into a complete thesis-builder layer.

Build the thesis artefact to preview it.
Part 5

Prompt Injection Risk Lab

This safe lab shows how untrusted text inside papers, websites, or copied notes can try to manipulate an AI research assistant. It teaches defensive reading, tool boundaries and source verification.

1. Injection simulation

The system will detect and quarantine suspicious instructions while preserving legitimate academic content.

Injection scan will appear here.
Cleaned academic content will appear here.
Part 6

Jailbreak Resilience Lab

This section demonstrates jailbreak categories defensively. Participants see how a robust research assistant refuses unsafe manipulation while continuing to help with legitimate scholarly tasks.

1. Defensive jailbreak tests

Select a test. The assistant explains the risk, refuses the unsafe instruction, and redirects to academically valid support.

Jailbreak resilience output will appear here.
